title: Materials & Technology
description: Classes of materials, their properties and applications. Metals, alloys, intermetallics, oxide and non-oxide ceramics, polymers, composites, semiconductors. Atomic, molecular, crystal structures and microstructure of materials. Crystalline (single, polycrystalline) and amorphous materials. Thin films, coatings. Structure-property correlations in materials. Processing of materials. Environmental degradation of materials. Status of metallurgical and materials industry in India. Overview of frontier areas in metallurgy and materials science.

reviews.body:
Note: This review was automatically scrapped from a DAMP blog. To read the complete review, kindly visit their site.

2020

Professor when you took the course:

1/3 – Prof. Anirban Patra, 2/3 – Nagamani Jaya Balila, 3/3 – Prof. R.P Shimpi

Motivation behind the course:

The course is quite fundamental in nature for any Materials Engineer. It deals with the strength properties of materials and expands upon the “Mechanical Properties of Solids” from JEE. Materials in any kind of engineering application – most of the times are placed under various kinds and magnitudes of stress – thus it is important to determine the amount of stress any kind of material would be placed under, in that engineering application and what would be the most optimum material to use in that application/ scenario – so that the material does not give way during use – leading to failure/ potential hazards to human life.

Course Content :

PROF. ANIRBAN PATRA
The course begins from the general definition of stress (A Tensor quantity) and the different types of stress – Tensile, Normal, Shear and the condition on the stress tensor under equilibrium. We only deal with objects in equilibrium in this course.

We then learn about strains – True Strain, Engineering Strain, Shear Strain and learn about Hooke’s Law and Linear Elasticity and Poisson’s Effect.

After this the course picks up pace and goes into stress transformations and Mohr’s Circle – generalized Hooke’s Law to strain transformations and stress and strain energy densities.
(Here Prof. Patra’s Part Ends, after which Prof. Jaya Balila takes over)

PROF. JAYA BALILA
We know dive into yielding properties of Materials – Von Mises and Tresca Criteria for Yielding and yield loci for Multi-Axial Stresses on a body and Normality Rule and Plasticity conditions of a material.

We now learn about Torsion as a mode of stress and how it is related to shear stress – Stress and Strain Equations for Torsion, Non-Uniform Torsion, Composite Bars and Hollow Circular Shafts.

After this we are introduced to some Shear Force and Bending Moment Problems – those who liked Mechanics and Laws of Motion during JEE would enjoy this part, we are given a wide variety of load distributions on a beam and we need to find out the loads and bending moments at each point on the beam in equilibrium conditions.
(After this, Prof. Jaya Balila’s part ends and Prof. Shimpi takes over)

PROF. SHIMPI
Prof. Shimpi expands upon the previous part of Bending Moment problems and introduces us to Elementary Beam Theory. In the previous part while solving Bending Moment problems we assumed that while we applied loads onto beams, the deflection of the beams could be ignored. With Elementary Beam Theory we try to calculate the deflections of the beams given the load distributions on the beams.

Prof. Shimpi concludes the course by taking up two more topics – viz Buckling of beams due to loads parallel to their axis and Thin Pressure Vessel Design.

Feedback : 

Prof. Anirban:
Prof. Anirban taught concepts pretty well and took things at a moderate pace. He was pretty approachable and cleared doubts well, he wrote things on the whiteboard in real time, consequently there weren’t any fully explained slides. rather the slides were notes in a way with formulae and derivations written – would be a bit hard to read just them and not attend class.

Prof. Jaya Balila:
She taught the parts involving Torsion and Bending Moments very well. The classes involved some problem solving too. I think she sort of rushed over the part involving the Yield Criteria and Theory of Plasticity of Materials (which is sort of counterintuitive since she taught those parts before Torsion and Bending Moments on beams). To be honest, for me, the hardest part in the course was the Theory of Plasticity and Yield Loci – I wasn’t able to wrap my head around that part in particular.

Prof. Shimpi
He actually is from the Department of Aerospace Engineering. It was nice to have him teach us. He takes his part slower than the other profs. Initially he repeated quite a lot of content over and over in his classes – which led to things progressing quite slowly. He teaches well too, but is slow. He explains things well and always welcomes class participation. Although his slides are made well so just reading from those would be sufficient. (Inside Joke : He always implored us to use our common sense (some of us are smarter than Euler XD) to find out flaws in the old models of Beam Theory.

Overall the course was quite interesting – for me this course was the most interesting course in Semester 4. There were 6 graded tutorials – the tutorials were discussed (by the TAs) at a certain time during the class hours and needed to be submitted at a later date. There was a 5% weightage to class participation during the Tutorial sessions. The Tutorials were quite easy and helped in understanding the subject.

Feedback on evaluation process : 

The evaluation process for the course comprised –
3 Quizzes (Total 10% – i.e 3.33% each)
6 Tutorials (15% Total)
Class Participation during Tutorials (5%)
Midsem (35%)
Endsem (35%)

The quizzes and tutorials were very scoring and very easy. The Midsem was on the tougher side and lengthy (questions were had many calculations
The Endsem – although not numerically involved had many questions asking for derivations of formulae and also the assumptions we made during the derivations and was somewhat lengthy – because there was a lot to answer. The professors were considerate enough and only asked the Post-Midsem portion for the Endsems and also reduced the weightage of endsems for the overall grading. Speed was sort of an important factor during the midsems and the endsems.

The latter part of the course involved many questions on Elementary Beam Theory and Bending Moment Theory – solving tutorials for this particular part is very essential and also paying attention in class when a few questions are solved in class are very important to be well aware of the boundary conditions to be applied (since we solve a differential equation for this part).
Slides were available for all the 3 parts and recordings were available for both the tutorial sessions and the lectures. Try to attend the lectures though since watching the recordings (atleast for me) takes up more time than the actual duration of the lecture XD. To be honest, the course (atleast for me) was on the easier side – since it just required us to solve the tutorials and questions of similar difficulty were asked in the exams – just try to focus extra during the Yield Criteria, ield Loci and Theory of Plasticity – that is the only part of the course which I found tough and unable to understand.

The TAs were very good and approachable – they were able to solve our doubts quite well! We had a Whatsapp group with the TAs where most of us asked our doubts.

Difficulty level :  2.5

How is the Grading?

The grading was relative – marks were scaled wrt the topper (for eg. the topper got 91.69 so his marks were scaled to 100 and others’ marks were multiplied by 100/91.69) and then the grades were allotted.

The grading was somewhat on the lenient side
AP – 2
AA – 7
AB – 29
BB – 16
BC – 12
CC – 23
CD – 13
DD – 3
FF – 1
FR – 1

Miscellaneous (e.g. Changes you felt as a result of the online mode):

There wasn’t really any problem I faced particular to an online sem – the TAs were very helpful. Although by the end of the course most of the students had stopped attending classes altogether, and also since Prof. Shimpi was very keen on interacting with students and it being an online mode of instruction did not really help much.

Thanks Kunind Sahu for writing the review !

2018

Professor when you took the course:

Prof. Aparna Singh

Motivation behind the course:

The course introduces to the basics of failure of a material under loading. It is an intensive, numerical based course which requires application of Mechanics.

Pre requisites:

Almost none. Good mechanics application to solve problems ( a skill students possess from their JEE days) would be a plus.

Feedback on tutorials, assignments and exams:

Tutorials and assignments are supplementary to class practice, it is always helpful to solve them, as they clear most of the concepts taught in class. Moreover some questions in the endsem were directly from the tutorials. Tutorials carried 10% weightage so you better not miss them!

The midsem was quite easy, but it was difficult to score in the quizzes and even more in  the endsem. The difficulty tends to increase as the course progresses.  It is difficult to keep up with the level if you’re not attending lectures regularly. Apart from that, a term paper presentation had to be done on one of the topics given by the professor.

Assignments and tutorials: 10%

2 quizzes: 10%

midsem  :20%

term paper presentation: 15%

Endsem : 40+5 % (Questions with additional 5% weightage will be from the term paper.)

Difficulty level:

Overall difficulty 3.5/5

It is moderately tough if you are not regular to lectures and tutorials. However, if you attend lectures you will find that the course is pretty easy.

Grading statistics:

Expect no more than a CC if you did not take the course seriously. The grading is not uniform, to say the least. It definitely isn’t a normal gaussian curve unlike other courses. More students are on the lesser end of the spectrum. FRs, DDs,CDs and CCs add together to almost 60 percent of the grades.

Cribbing after paper evaluation is generally very difficult and unrewarding, as the instructor is very strict about the marking scheme – including the final answer.

Study Material and References:

Mechanics of Material by Gere and Timoshenko

Mechanics of Solids , Crandall, Dahl, Lardner, Sivakumar.

Thanks to Sumant Wasule for writing this course review

Professors:

Prof. Parag Bhargava and Prof. Prasanna

Motivation behind the course:

As the name suggests this course is designed to give a basic exposure to the experimental techniques that are available for the characterization of materials. The goal of the course is to get students acquainted with basic characterization technique and enable them to quantify material properties like density, surface area, organic-inorganic content, conductivity, morphology, porosity etc.. In the words of Prof. Parag Bhargava -“This course give us the power to know what a material is made of? ”

Course content:


Click here to continue reading

Instructor

Prof. Parag Bhargava

Section

Default allotment by the institute

Semester

Autumn

Course Difficulty

Moderate to difficult course. Reason for the categorisation: the course is very interesting but attending classes regularly and following the professor carefully becomes almost essential to score good in the course, lack of good slides is also a reason. Alternatively you can follow the book William Callister’s Material Science and Engineering, but even after sincerely doing all of that you can’t be assured of a good grade, that’s what makes this course difficult. I’ll cover more about the examination a little later in the review.

Time Commitment Required

Not very time-consuming if you have attended the lectures and sincerely paid attention, even the project is performed in groups of around 10 members which eases off the load from individual members. But without attending lectures, the course may become very demanding before examinations.

Grading

Grading was relative and was done leniently.

Attendance Policy

Attendance was not mandatory, but for this specific course, very important

Pre-requisites

None

Evaluation Scheme and Weightages

  • Mid-semester - 30 marks
  • Project - 20 marks
  • End-semester - 50 marks

Topics Covered in the Course

To be updated

Quality of Lectures

Prof. Bhargava is one of those professors who focuses more on building an engineer over teaching a student. He gives a lot of information in class from his latest research and problems faced in the industry related to the topic being taught, the only problem is the sheer size of the knowledge he shares in the class, there is no way it can all be covered in slides, thus the slides alone aren’t very useful. He speaks out of his experience, and expects students to listen carefully and to critically think during exams. Two lectures of Prof. Bhargava you would never want to miss. The very first lecture when he talks about why you should become an engineer and the first lecture after your mid-semester examinations; the purpose of life lecture. One of the best professors, I have interacted with so far in life.

Assignments and projects in the Course

Prof. Parag Bhargava offers a choice between an Industrial visit __ project (will update it). Students had to report their learnings and present it in front of the class. Questions from the presentation were asked in the end-semester examination. The highest overall scores in the course hovers around 60%, and the project is one place where you can easily fetch marks, that alone if done sincerely should be sufficient to pass the course. You may want to take it very seriously.

Exams

  • Mid-semester - moderate to difficult
  • Project - Easy
  • End-semester - Moderate to difficult

The exam is an interesting element in this course, it may seem like anything and everything related to engineering metallurgy is being asked in the exam, questions like, explain the material and manufacturing process of the pen you are writing the exam with, I hope you understood. On Prof. Bhargava’s standards, the question above will fall in the easy category, he asks some very involved questions in the exam which requires the student to critically think, the most elegant feature of his examination question is that you don’t need any more knowledge than what is covered in the class to score a 100% in his exam still the highest that anyone scores is around 60%.

Reference Material

  • William Callister’s Material Science and Engineering

The best reference material would be the class topper’s notes, the second best would be rely on your friend’s who regularly attend classes. You can also consult a few seniors, use their notes if they were regular in class. Only if both of you were taught by prof. Bhargava.

Importance of Course

Very important, foundation to all kinds of projects that students may be involved in. It eases understanding of concepts in higher level courses: Manufacturing Process 1 and Manufacturing Process 2. It touches upon all the basics of Engineering Metallurgy that an engineer should know, not restricted to Mechanical Engineers.

Review by: Arvind Manimurugan

The motivation behind the course:

This course, in brief, is all about the collection of data, its analysis, and drawing conclusions and inferences. It aims to familiarize students with the applications of Data Science in real-time, particularly usage of Statistics in the Engineering domain. The main objectives are to understand the data collection methodology and to draw conclusions from data and to know the errors/assumptions involved.

Course Content:

The course kicks off with basic concepts of Probability, Random Variables and Special Random Variables. JEE knowledge of Probability and Statistics is sufficient to understand the Pre-Midsem portion. The course speeds up a little bit after the mid-semester and includes Distributions of sampling statistics, Parameter estimation, Hypothesis testing, Regression, Analysis of variance, Goodness of ﬁt test and Nonparametric hypothesis tests.

One very interesting aspect of this course is that it includes R tutorials and sessions for the analysis of data. All the theory taught is used in the analysis of data provided using the open-source software, R. The Professors and TAs are quite enthusiastic to solve the doubts if any.

title: Structure of Materials
description: Crystalline, amorphous and other materials (liquid crystals, quasicrystals, 2D materials); Concept of symmetry and crystals (lattice + motif) and translational periodicity; Bravais lattices, unit cells and primitive cells. Introduction to point groups and space groups. Fractional coordinates, crystallographic directions and planes in cubic and non-cubic systems. Interplanar spacings and angles, zone axes. Review of common crystal structures in metals (fcc, bcc, hcp) and ceramics (rock salt, zinc blende, wurtzite, spinel, perovskite, etc), ordered structures and notations, network solids (silica, silicates, etc); Pauling’s rules; Stereographic projection; reciprocal lattice; crystal defects - point defects in metallic and ionic crystals (Kroger-Vink notation), dislocations, Burgers vector and its representation. Planar defects: stacking faults, twins, grain boundaries; defects in ordered phases, microstructure of materials; Structure of glasses and amorphous materials; influence of bonding, structure, defects and microstructure on physical properties.

Course review


Details

  • Year : 2023

Prerequisites necessary (if any)

No prerequisites as such (A general idea of Solid State (JEE) and Miller Indices (if taught in MM105) might help)


Course difficulty

Difficulty Level: 4/5

The course overall requires visualization to a great extent. The papers were not easy and require thorough understanding of the concepts beforehand. There were quizzes held in class every alternate week. All the exams were of True/False, MCQs and select the correct statements type during our time (Note: The prof changes the paper pattern every year). Plane groups, Space groups, XRD are the topics on which one must spend sufficient time and clear the concepts if they have doubts asap. The exams become more tricky as there is negative marking in all the exams which were conducted in this course.

Recommend to be attentive in class and understand concepts in the class itself, this might help you for an effective and better revision during your study time.


Grading Scheme:

Attendance: 5%

Quizzes: 15%

Midsem: 35%

Endsem: 45%


The grading was Moderate with 10 AAs and 12-13 ABs given during our time.


Personal opinion

I consider this course as one of the best courses I have undertook in the department. It is a course which is purely concept based and does not involve lot of formulas/computations which is a convention of most of the courses offered by the department. I insist all of you to enjoy this course and not get intimidated by the general notion of it.

Examples from materials processing: CVD/PVD reactors, thin film deposition,. combustion, oxidation/reduction, drying, calcinations, precipitation etc.

Professor when you took the course:

Prof. Nirdosh K Khosla (Pre Midsems) & Prof Somnath Basu (Post Midsems)

Motivation behind the course:

This course covers the elementary processes for extraction, separation and processing of metal from their minerals. All the processes are only briefly touched upon throughout the course without going too much in depth. The aim of this course is to equip the student with the quantitative aspects of mineral processing & process metallurgy.

Course Content:

The course is majorly divided into two parts: Mineral Processing (pre midsems) & Process Metallurgy (post midsems). As a part of mineral processing, Initially the course starts with the basics of fluid dynamics and concepts like viscosity along with mass balance and volume balance in flowing fluids. Economics aspects of ore refining and smelting pay charts are touched upon. Concepts of Particle shape and particle size determination and size reduction methods are also covered. Separation techniques like thickeners, cyclones, filters, jigging & froth flotation are discussed in detail along with problem solving methods.

title: Phase Transformations
description: Free energy - composition diagrams for binary systems. Diffusion in interstial and substitutional solid solutions. Atomic mobility and vacancy diffusion. High diffusivity paths. Kirkendall effect. Diffusion in ceramics, Applications of diffusion equations. Homogeneous and heterogeneous nucleation in a single component system. Interface and diffusion controlled growth. Coherent, semicoherent and incoherent interfaces. Transformations controlled by heat flow. Solidification of a single phase alloy. Dendritic and cellular growth. Kinetics of eutectic and eutectoid transformations, precipitation and dispersion hardening. Cold work, recovery and recrystallization. Grain growth. Order-disorder changes. Elements of martensitic transformation. Hardening and tempering phenomena.

Professor when you took the course: Prof. Nithyanand Prabhu

Motivation for the course: To familiarize students with various types of phase transformations and explain the science that governs these processes in terms of thermodynamics, kinetics and interfaces

Course Content: This particular course is an 8 credit course and is tough to be honest. It requires continuous effort from the students side in order to score a good grade in this course. As far as the evaluation is concerned, the professor took a midsem of 30%, endsem of 50% and rest of the 20% was distributed among 4 quizzes and 5 assignments. the questions asked involved performing numerical calculations, doing derivations and explaining certain concepts covered in class. It is important to stay regular in this course.

The course is broadly divided into three parts – Thermodynamics, Kinetics and Interfaces.The Thermodynamics part covers the construction of phase diagrams using free energy curves, chemical potential concept, Gibbs’ phase rule and basic mathematical formulations in phase transformations. The Kinetics part starts with diffusion theory in terms of Fick’s first and second laws and then uses this theory to explain the role of diffusion in phase transformations. Additionally, it also talks about diffusion-less transformations. Lastly, the interfaces part describes how boundaries play a role in phase transformations. Also, towards the end of the course, the professor explains certain specific phase transformations using the above concepts covered earlier

Pre requisites: No prerequisites required. However, brushing up the Gibbs Free Energy diagrams and phase diagrams covered in the Thermodynamics course might be helpful

Feedback on Lectures and Teaching: Prof. Prabhu is highly enthusiastic about this course and puts in his heart and soul to ensure that the students are able to understand, follow and comprehend the slides during the lecture. He is willing to answer even the silliest of doubts asked by students and can explain a particular concept over and over again until the students completely understand it. He does expect active participation from the students during classes as well, so it is extremely useful to attend classes regularly and take notes.

Professor when you took the course:
Prof. K. Narasimhan & Prof. Indradev Samajdar

Motivation behind the course:
To understand plastic forming of metals through a detailed study of key metal forming techniques and their real-world applications in the manufacturing of products.

Course Content:
The course is divided into 2 parts – Pre-midsem (Prof. K. Narasimhan) & Post-midsem (Prof. Indradev Samajdar). The pre-midsem part deals with the technology behind the important metal forming operations. The primary forming processes covered include rolling, extrusion, forging, wire drawing and sheet-metal forming. The post-midsem part deals with ‘Case Studies’, wherein the thermo-mechanical processing of some important products is analysed. Examples include aluminium beverage cans, steel for car body applications & zirconium alloys for the nuclear industry.

Pre requisites: 
MM 203 – Mechanics of Materials

Feedback on lectures: 
Attending lectures is important to get a clear understanding of the course contents. As far as the attendance scheme is considered, manual attendance is taken, with no explicit restriction on the minimum attendance required.

Feedback on tutorials, assignments and exams: 
Both the parts (pre-midsem & post-midsem) include a couple or more tutorials, a quiz and an exam (mid-sem for the pre-midsem part & end-sem for the post-midsem part). The tutorials are a great resource for understanding the key concepts, with similar questions being asked in the exams. The relative weightage of each quiz and exam is generally 15 % and 35 % respectively, with tutorials having very less or negligible weightage.

Course Content: First time conducted in the online mode, this 8 credit course is divided into 2 parts. The first part, Iron making involves the entire metallurgical process from ore to hot metal. It is a good mix of a lot of theory and numericals based on thermodynamic concepts (mass and heat balance, ellingham diagram, adiabatic flame temperature). Steel making picks up from where we left off at the end of the first half and deals with the prominent methods of producing steel in India. The course uses a lot of concepts taught in Thermodynamics and Kinetics of processes.

Feedback on Lectures and Teaching: As this was the first time delivering online lectures, we were given about 2 hours of recorded lectures and 1 hour of doubt clearing session every week.

Feedback on evaluation process: We had a lot of continuous evaluation. 7 quizzes of approximately 5 marks each on average, 5 marks for 4 assignments (given every 2 weeks) and a 13 mark midsem to cap off the 50 marks for the first half. The second half consisted of best 3/4 quizzes (30 marks), 5 marks for assignments and a 15 mark endsem exam. All exams were conducted on Moodle.

Difficulty level: 3.5

How is the Grading?
Normal distribution is followed with very few AAs ABs. So grading is a bit tough. Not a lot of FRs, probably owing to the professor being lenient as it was online for the first time.

Professor when you took the course:
Prof. Smrutiranjan Parida ,Prof. Vngaranahalli S. Raja

Motivation behind the course:
Corrosion is a huge industry currently and research is still going on because the economic losses due to corrosion are humongous .  A lot of problems still need to be solved and the research till date in this industry is not as much as compared to other material science fields. This course can really be the platform for a student to develop interest in this field and pursue further studies in corrosion of materials.

Course Content:
Includes Corrosion Rate Determination, Electrochemical cell setup and measurement of potentials and pH, Determination of Electrochemical Corrosion Parameters using potentiodynamic polarization technique, Intergranular Corrosion  Test and Study of weldments using Scanning Electrochemical Microscope

Pre requisites: If you know the basics of electrochemistry it would be really helpful in the beginning portion of the course but no pre requisites required as such

Feedback on lectures: 
This course runs parallel to MM 454 (Corrosion and Protection of Materials) and most of the experiments we perform are based on the content taught in the lectures of MM 454. If you are attentive in the lectures and read the slides properly almost 75 per cent of your work is completed for the lab also. No separate lectures for this lab are taken.

Feedback on tutorials, assignments and exams: 
There were a total of 4 experiments (and one DEMO experiment) and a viva is taken in each of them. The report has to be submitted on the next turn before the lab starts. Make sure that you are completing the experiments honestly and report your findings properly. This is very important because your concepts need to be really strong for the vivas. The endsem was partly objective with multiple choice questions and partly subjective.  Weightage : Endsem -40 , Reports + viva +attendance -60

description: Lectures • Introduction • Introduction to Piezoelectricity and Ferroelectricity • Crystal structure and symmetry considerations in ferro/piezoelectrics • Historical review of development of theories of piezo- and ferro-electricity • Theory of ferroelectricity • Phenomenological (thermodynamic) theory of ferroelectricity • First and second order ferroelectrics • Soft mode theory of ferroelectricity • Ferroelectric domains and their switching • Anti-ferroelectrics • Relaxor ferroelectrics, nanopolar regions and dielectric dispersion • Tensor representation of various physical properties • Theory of piezoelectricity • Piezoelectricity vs. Electrostriction • Equations of state and various co-efficients in piezoelectricity • Measurement Techniques in Ferro/Piezoelectrics • Dielectric measurements • Polarisation measurements • Piezoelectric measurements • Applications: ◦ High permittivity dielectrics ◦ Ferroelectric memory devices ◦ FeRAM ◦ FeFET-based memory ◦ FTJ-based memory ◦ Pressure sensors/Accelerometers/Gyroscopes ◦ Ultrasonic Transducers ◦ Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW) devices ◦ Piezoelectric Transformers ◦ Piezoelectric Actuators ◦ Electro-optic Devices ◦ Photoferroelectric devices ▪ Photovoltaics ▪ Photodetectors ▪ Photocatalysts ◦ Energy harvesting: ▪ Piezoelectric mechanical energy harvesting ▪ Pyroelectric thermal energy harvesting ◦ Dielectric capacitors for energy storage: ▪ Antiferroelectrics ▪ Relaxor ferroelectrics

reviews.body:
How did you find out about this course, and what led you to take it?

I was on the look-out for a good department elective and came across this one. Also, one of the primary reasons I took this course is because of the professor as he had earlier taken a core course for us during the sophomore year and was pretty chill in nature.

Could you briefly explain the curriculum of this course?

The course first delves into tensor transformation theories to build the basic math required for understanding such materials. The prof also goes into point groups and stereographic projections. Then the curriculum concerns itself with the various theories of piezoelectricity and ferroelectricity, ending with their respective applications in today's world.

How is the rigor of this course, and what was the weekly effort?

The prof provides the slides for this course, and it is one of the lowest effort courses I have done here in insti. The prof explains complex theories in such an easy way and also the slides of this course are curated in such a way that it becomes super easy for a student to understand. I would say studying before the day of the exam and attending classes would do more than enough. Even if you miss a class the slides are so comprehensive you can very well understand from it and ask doubts to the prof next class. The prof is very helpful in solving doubts in an easy manner.

How was the exam pattern and evaluation?

The exams contained only two types of questions: Numerical and True/False with reasons. The quiz and mid sem was closed book and the end sem was open-notes. Relevant formulae were provided in the question paper so there was no need to memorise them. There also was no attendance policy in this course.
The breakup of the marks is as follows:

  • Quiz: 15%
  • Midsem: 30%
  • Research Paper review Assignment: 15%
  • End Sem: 40%

How was the grading of the course? And was it easy to score?

The grading was good with 8 AA/ABs out of 15 students registered for the course. It was very easy to score in the course as the questions came directly from the slides and some involved just value substituting to arrive at the answer.

What were the pros/cons of this course? Any suggestions for people wanting to take this course?

If you are looking for a STEM elective/ Dept elective and want it to be relatively easy and having very good grading, this is the course for you. I see no cons for taking this course.

How did this course help you, and/or how is it relevant for your career?

This course is really helpful for people who want to pursue materials science in the future as it delves into the basic understanding of piezo and ferro materials and also discusses their application. This course comes under the BSBE minor and also helped me to practice reading a lot of research articles, a huge pro of this course.

List of Pursued Courses Mentioned

  • MM6002: Principles and Applications of Piezoelectric and Ferroelectric Materials (This course is part of the BSBE minor)

reviews.body:
Note: This review was automatically scrapped from a DAMP blog. To read the complete review, kindly visit their site.

Professor when you took the course:
Prof. Prita Pant

Motivation behind the course:
Thin film materials are often used for their electrical (conductor lines in integrated circuits), optical (reflecting or absorbing layers), magnetic (memory devices) or catalytic properties. In many of these applications the films are subjected to very high stresses, which could lead to either degradation or failure of the film. In order to understand, predict, and improve the performance of thin film materials and devices it is necessary to understand their mechanical properties. The goal of this course is to give students an appreciation of how stresses arise in films so that depending on the application (and intended life of the film) they can be controlled. While the students would benefit from a basic idea of stresses and strains and crystal structures, it is not essential provided the student is willing to put in extra effort to understand these concepts during the course. It is modeled on the course taken by Prof W.D. Nix of Stanford University and used in a graduate course on Mechanical Properties of Thin Films.

Course Content:
>Origins of stresses in thin films. >Basics of stress, strain and elasticity â€“ applied to thin film geometry (tensors and anisotropic elasticity) >Measurement of stresses (Curvature) and strains (X-Ray) in thin films >Experimental methods for measuring mechanical properties of thin films (nanoindentation, micro-cantilever beam bending, micro-tensile test, bulge test, micropillar compression etc.) >Some models used to explain the mechanical behaviour of thin films > Stoney’s equation, Thin film growth mechanism (more emphasis on Volmer-Weber growth mechanism) and stresses associated during growth.

Pre requisites: 
No prerequisite needed. Some basic knowledge of Mechanics (MM 203) and XRD (MM201, MM206) will be enough.

Feedback on lectures: 
No attendance criteria. However, the student should be present in the lecture hall within the first 10mins. Class participation is important, as there are no books published for this. It might get a bit tough to catch up, if you miss a lecture. Pace of lectures vary depending upon the requirement of the students.

Feedback on tutorials, assignments and exams: 
Tutorials and assignments will help to give you a better and realistic idea about the thin film and it’s associated processes,stresses. Most of the tutorials will be related to some research paper and what you’ll be doing is that you’ll verify the values given in the paper. The exams will be SLIGHTLY more difficult than the tutorials. Tut/assgn will be graded and will have around 7-8% weightage. You’ll also have a paper presentation towards the end, which account for 10% weightage. The 10% weightage will distributed as 7+3 . 7% for your presentation and 3% for participation during presentations.

reviews.body:
Instructor

Tanushree Chaudhury

Semester

Autumn ‘21

Course Difficulty

Easy to Moderate

Time Commitment Required

Around 4-5 hours per week

Grading Policy and Statistics

Grading: Not so great :/
Statistics: Refer to ASC for details. No one got an AA but everyone was above BC with majority getting BB.

Attendance Policy

None, but one is required to submit 80% of the evaluations done in this course

Pre-requisites

No hard prerequisites as such. Some background about semiconductors from EE 101 and preliminaries about nuclei formation from ME 221 will help.

Evaluation Scheme

Homework: 20
Midsem Viva + Take home endsem: 20
Participation: 10
Term Paper: 50

Topics Covered in the Course

  1. Overview of applications of electrical and magnetic materials – case studies
  2. Bonding, Crystalline structure, microstructure, and the impact thereof
  3. Band structure, E-k diagrams, materials classification
  4. Metals: thermal and electrical properties, effect of composition and microstructures
  5. Semiconductors: Conductivity, doping, p-n junctions, E-x diagram application, optical properties: implications of E-k diagrams, band engineering, heterostructures, synthesis of heterostructures.
  6. Insulators: dielectric properties in static and dynamic fields
  7. Ferroelectric materials: spontaneous polarization, crystal structure correlation, piezoelectricity, pyroelectricity, impact of processing parameters
  8. Magnetic materials: basics, ordering, ferro-, dia-, para-, magnetic materials

Due to time constraints, the instructor could not cover topics 6, 7 and 8, so it became just an ‘Electrical Materials’ course :p
Major part of the course was focused on topic no. 5

Teaching Style

The instructor used MSTeams and significant weightage was given to in-class participation.

Tutorials/Assignments/Projects

There were 5 homework assignments throughout the course which were somewhat time consuming. The term paper had a weightage of 50% and the instructor floated a few topics for the same (for eg. transparent solar cells, sensors, etc). Teams of 2 were formed and the goal was to suggest better materials for the selected application. Basically, it was a literature review based on which we had to suggest our improvements.

Feedback on Exams

There was no midsem but she took a viva and asked basic questions about the content. The endsem was in take-home mode with just one simple question and 24 hours to submit

Motivation for taking this course

To get more deeper insights into magnetism in matter than what was taught during JEE, but sadly that was not covered in the course.

Course Highlights

The course starts with preliminary solid state physics (more or less same as in ME 221) and then moves on to semiconductors, bandstructures, carrier densities and the effect of external parameters on the carrier densities (like temperature or doping). The instructor spent a fair amount of time on this topic. Meanwhile, there were regular discussions about the term paper during the class hours. Perhaps this is why there were time constraint issues at the end. After the midsem, a lot of time was devoted to learning synthesis techniques for semiconductors/heterostructures and the theoretical aspects associated with those.
Overall, the content was decent and the instructor’s teaching was quite good. Since this was the first time she took the course, she made modifications on the way. One can expect a more organized MM 685 in the coming semesters with a well-defined flow.

Course Importance

This course can provide decent exposure to synthesis techniques for semiconductors, transistors, other electrical devices if one is interested to work in companies like Intel, TSMC, etc. We had a guest lecture where the instructor’s friend from Intel talked about her experience. The course will open avenues in materials research as well.

How strongly would I recommend this course?

On a scale of 0-10 with 0 being don’t ever and 10 being a strong yes; a 7

When to take this course?

I took it in my 7th semester. There is no ideal time as such, but I’d suggest taking this after EE 101 and ME 221 to have a better background.

References Used

Recommended textbook is Electronic, Magnetic and Optical Materials by Pradeep Fulay

Motivation behind this course:
This course makes you understand the fundamentals of organic semiconductors (bonding, conjugation, hybridization, electronic structure) gradually moving towards their processing techniques such as patterning, printing etc. The course covers various electro-optical devices such as thin film transistors, photovoltaics in brief.

Prerequisites:
Not really, remembering organic chemistry basics from 1st year courses should surely help though.

Course content:
3 main aspects that the course covers:-
Fundamental of organic semiconductors
• Bonding, Conjugation, Hybridization
• Electronic structure
• Charge injection and transport
• Optical phenomenon (light emission/absorption)
Materials and their processing
• Small molecules, Polymers, p – or n-type
• Film deposition techniques
• Structure-property relationships
• Patterning, Printing, Encapsulation
Electro-optical devices
• Organic Light emitting diodes
• Organic thin film transistors
• Organic solar cells
• Organic Biosensors

reviews.body:
Note: This review was automatically scrapped from a DAMP blog. To read the complete review, kindly visit their site.

Professor when you took the course: Course instructor is Prof. Shobha Shukla, but the lectures were taught by Prof. Donald McNaughton from Monash University.

The motivation behind the course: 

Vibrational Spectroscopy techniques are a handy tool to investigate different avenues of research. Knowledge of these techniques and how they work will be convenient when doing hands-on work in the field. These techniques are almost always used in the labs which are part of core courses for UG students but somehow, the TA’s never seem to be able to explain precisely how they work. Students brush through the experiment without actually understanding what they have done. For those people, this course is a blessing.

This course explores less talked about techniques in Materials Science research. People in the department mostly talk about XRD or SEM because these are the only ones we are properly exposed to. However, knowledge of some other techniques is always helpful. This course also aims to motivate students to possibly explore newer techniques, not in the course and even design techniques of their own. Instrumentation forms a small part of the course as well, which was interesting.

Course Content:

The course covers different modes of motion of the molecule and ways to detect and classify them. Then it goes on to cover Infrared spectroscopy principles, theory and different configurations, Raman spectroscopy and different configurations, comparisons between Raman and IR spectra, ways to enhance and refine spectra, and finally a brief glance at Synchrotron spectroscopy. The entire course was filled with real-life examples of applications of these techniques, which makes it engaging.

Which topic did you choose to do the SLP in? What was your motivation behind it?

Computational astrophysics

My project was with Prof. Varun Bhalerao, and my work was based on the polarimetry of gamma ray bursts with the upcoming Daksha space telescope. I did the SLP in my 5th semester. I started working with Prof. Bhalerao in my 3rd semester, through an iSURP. The project started off as something else but eventually evolved into the simulations and the development of a Python pipeline for detecting the polarization of GRBs.
As such I did not “choose” the topic as it was a natural continuation of my long-term research project with the professor, and it continued after the SLP as well. The motivation was basically to credit my work, and I would have done the same amount of work anyways had the SLP not been there.

Structure of the said SLP.

There was a final report (~20 pages long) submission and a 20 minute presentation. The timelines can vary across departments and might be different every sem, but one caveat in my time was that the report was demanded before endsems, which was unusual and pretty inconvenient. It looks like this has become the norm in the physics department at least. The presentation was after endsems.
No prerequisites as such, and I just asked my professor about taking this up before registering which he readily agreed to, under one condition – I keep track of the deadlines myself and inform him of the same.

Overview of what all a student should expect to gain from it.

The SLP just adds a formal structure to your research project, and gives you some free credits. It is a good way to start off with research, and something you should do as soon as possible if you are serious about going into the field. The report writing experience is good exposure to scientific writing and also good LaTeX practice (please use LaTeX for this). You should try to involve the prof in the writing process and ask them to critique initial drafts and give inputs. You can also practice presenting in a group meeting or similar setting with seniors/grad students if possible, as presenting your research in a succinct and concise manner is a skill different from the typical stuff we do for course projects.

Words of Wisdom

If you are already working with a prof, through an SURP/iSURP or otherwise, don’t hesitate to ask them for an SLP right away. Most profs would be happy to let you credit the work you’re doing, and the expectations for an SLP are quite low. I was not aware of this, but even a semester’s worth of reading up on your topic or familiarizing yourself with the field is enough for an SLP. You don’t need to do a lot. Your SLP can just be a literature review – if the prof agrees.
If your project is long term, this paves the way to do a BTP with the same prof early on – a BTP can be done even in the third year. I did my SLP after two full semesters of work for some reason, which wasn’t the smartest idea. By the time I did my SLP, I had enough progress and material to do a BTP if I wanted to.
Lastly, please take the project and the opportunity given by the professor seriously and put in a good amount of effort throughout the semester. A lot of people somehow start working a week before the deadline and just package anything they can into a report. This defeats the purpose of a research project and is definitely not how you want to do it.

reviews.body:
Session – 2018-19

Instructor – Sai Vinjanampathy

Course Content –

The course started with the professor giving a brief overview of Quantum Mechanics. He expected that students have some prior experience in basic quantum mechanics although it was not a very hard prerequisite. He then taught the mathematics required to build the quantum mechanics formalism. He pressed on the importance of building the mathematical framework to understand quantum mechanics better. We then spent a few lectures on square wells, harmonic oscillator, 1D potentials, and scattering, tunneling, and transmission problems. We then covered some advanced topics like two-level systems, angular momentum, Laplace-Runge-Lenz vector, and addition of angular momentum. The prof also briefly touched some topics for heightening student’s curiosity. We ended with the addition of angular momentum, and QM2 was supposed to start from here.

Prerequisite –
Basic knowledge from MA 106 and PH 107

Feedback on the lectures –
We sat with first-year MSc students for this course. We had two 1.5 hours of lectures a week and an additional 1.5 hours tutorial on some weeks. The prof was very clear on his fundamentals. The lectures were slow in the beginning as the professor wanted everyone to be on the same page. The professor tried to keep the students involved and encouraged them to ask doubts. However, there were a few times when he couldn’t explain a topic well enough, but the professor made sure that he wouldn’t be testing the students on those topics.

Feedback on Assignments/Tutorials/Exams –

We had around 5 classes of tutorials in which we discussed a few problems from the assignments given. The professor sometimes also used the tutorials to teach extra topics.

3

Standard QM textbooks can be used but none were followed in the course

None

Future extensions to this course is QM 2 and 3.

reviews.body:
This course was audited by the reviewee.



Brief summary of the course and prerequisites (if any).

It was taught by Professor Varun Bhalerao, who is excellent at teaching and designing exams as well - he made the course a lot of fun. The course logistics were a little unusual - a 20% endsem and a 40% project, 10% interaction marks (awarded to you if you contributed to the collective course notes, and everyone got a turn to do so), and a formula sheet which necessarily was not allowed derivations or theory. This different structure was somewhat challenging but also very engaging and rewarding.
The course briefly covered various aspects of astronomy and instrumentation, and gave a short introduction to stellar astrophysics, along with a guest lecture on cosmology. It also taught basic photometry, which we were required to use for the project. It had no prerequisites.
The project was also very interesting - we had to come up with a detailed proposal for a target object and what parameters of it we would attempt to measure. We then applied for images from the Growth India Telescope - not archive data, but images taken during the semester, at the time and location we requested. We then had a few images we had to perform photometry on and calculate various parameters from the same. The project was more rigorous than most course projects and tried to give us a taste of what real research is like. The project evaluation also had a peer-grading component which forced us to pay attention to others’ project presentations, and exposed us to the many applications of photometry.

Motivation to Audit this course rather than taking it as an elective and the procedures to follow to apply for the course and complete auditing the course.

I had already taken up many courses that semester but was tempted to take this course as well - also, many of my peers were taking it this year and preferred to take the course with them rather than next year (especially due to the group project component).
I simply applied for the course as an Audit on ASC (during adjustment) and emailed the professor that I would be joining the course from the next class.
Later on, I discovered that auditing students were still required to take part in all evaluations - quizzes, the project, the endsem, all of them. To my knowledge this is not generally a requirement, but it was for this course. This set-up makes the audit slightly more challenging, since you can’t miss a quiz like you could afford to in other audit courses.

Rigor of the course as an audit course compared to a normal course.

I took the course quite seriously, except for the time I allotted it during midsems and endsems. I like to make detailed TeX-based notes (using Notion) for courses, and I did so for this one as well, so during the semester it did not feel less heavy than other courses. The project also took a fair bit of effort, especially since we chose a slightly challenging goal.
However, I could afford to not prepare much for the exams, which allowed me to focus on my core courses in what was (for me) an academically heavy semester. This was the main advantage of taking the course as an audit.
During the semester, I felt like since I was putting in so much effort anyways - and was able to manage my workload - I would prefer to have the course be an elective instead. I requested the dean to allow me to change the tag in the middle of the semester, but I was not given permission to do so.
While I got an AU grade, I emailed the professor and he did inform me as to what letter grade I would have gotten based on my evaluations.

Advice regarding auditing a course vs taking it up normally.

An audited course has the risk of getting neglected compared to your other courses. You need to be careful that you give the course the time it needs, else it is a waste of your time and the two audits you are allowed.
I think the main advantage of an audit is that you can prioritize other evaluations over its own safely. An audit should not be taken with the expectation that you will not attend classes - there is no point to having an audit in that case, it isn’t worth as much on the transcript as you might think it is.
It is also important to clarify with the professor what their audit requirements are going to be before the adjustment period ends. If you feel like you will need to put in a similar amount of work for the audit as for an elective, judge whether you can handle it and ditch the course or tag it an elective (or ALC, if the grading isn’t promising).
Most importantly, be clear on what you intend to do with the audit course. An audit is only useful if you want to learn the topic - for that, you will need to put in effort. Do not take audits because they feel like an easy compromise of learning but not putting in too much effort. You must be certain about how much effort you intend to put in and whether that is manageable and worth it.

Instructor: Prof. Varun Bhalerao

Session: 2019-2020

Prerequisites: N/A, but a fair understanding of basic physics would help.

Course content and structure:

The course aims to give an introduction to astrophysics. It covered topics like telescopes, working of detectors, gravitational waves, distance measurement techniques, coordinate systems, stellar classification, Gamma-Ray Bursts, X-Ray phenomena, spectral analysis of stars, basic cosmology, opacity, etc. In addition to theory, the course also involved a group course project of our choice, for which we were supposed to write observational proposals for GROWTH-India Telescope (Hanle) for getting the data. A session on how to use images from GROWTH was also held, which gave an introduction to image reduction and data analysis. Unfortunately, due to maintenance issues at Hanle and the COVID-19 pandemic, the projects had to be cancelled. Lighter tasks, like observing Venus over a period of time, trying to find out its orbital parameters, calculating the distance to the moon by parallax were floated, which were not graded, but contributed to a better understanding of the theory taught.

Feedback on lectures: The lectures were very interactive, with the professor constantly asking us questions and encouraging us to answer them on our own. The environment was on the informal side and often, discussions on current events in Astronomy (e.g. the dimming of Betelgeuse) were encouraged. Emphasis was placed on observational astronomy in the beginning of the course, which then went on to more theoretical stuff. There were guest lectures as well, by people with varied research interests.

Feedback on tutorials and exams: 

The weightage was as follows: 30% midsem, 30% project, 40% endsem.
The midsem was on the easier side, with numericals and points for diagrams. It was graded fairly and partial points were given for correct approach. It was lengthy as it had some calculations which can easily go wrong.

Difficulty (on a scale of 1 being very easy to 5 being very hard): 3

Textbooks & References: Carroll & Ostlie, Astronomy OpenStax, a lot of different online resources

Grading Statistics: The prof gave an S grade to all, as only the midsem was conducted.

Additional Comments & what you learnt from the course: Overall, the course was quite well structured, with proper resources available. In addition to that, one of the students had to upload that day’s lecture notes on Moodle. The course gave a solid foundation for the various fields in astronomy and provided interaction with post-docs with different research interests. It also gave us a basic idea on how to write proposals for a project.

Review by – Pranav Page (pranavpage33@gmail.com)

Teaching Style

Sir ensured that the 1.5h lectures were not monotonous at all. While the primary medium of instruction was using slides (+ notes), sir would use interactive simulation and graphic softwares to explain the topics better. He would actively ask us about updates on our projects and would take up discussions on general astrophysics as well. The lectures were all well planned and the resources were shared in a very organised way on Moodle.

For the second half of the course, sir called in speakers from various places (IUCAA, Caltech, etc.) to give lectures on topics they specialise in. This gave a very rounded flavour of astrophysics and were also very enjoyable.

Tutorials/Assignments/Projects

Sir took a total of 2-3 classes of problem solving where he would also solve the problems with us. This way, he did not go too fast and also ensured that we arrived till the final answers.

The assignments were mainly drawing graphs and plots (light curve of a binary or orbit of an asteroid) based on real astrophysical data and interpreting them in terms of errors, orbital parameters and so on. He would ensure that each student got a unique celestial body so that there was no copying (although, discussing approachers were highly encouraged).

The project was a major component of the course. It involved studying a class of objects in astrophysics and then coming up with a proposal to observe a single object from that class using the Growth India Telescope. We had to perform feasibility checks and also report the number of images we would need. Then, we had to use various data analysis and photometry tools to obtain the magnitudes and post process the information to reach out goal (for instance, to calculate the distance and approximate temperature of the star).

Midsem and endsem (since both had the same format)
The midsem was open notes (handwritten only) and had questions that required the students to make appropriate assumptions and inferences. All the questions were real life application based problems and generally had multiple subparts (so it helped for partial marking). It was focussed on numericals but also had a few conceptual questions.

Motivation for taking this course

Being a part of IITBSSP, I had an inclination towards space science. We had also covered orbital mechanics in the team and it developed my interest to explore further. Having heard really good reviews of the course and the instructor, I decided opt for it.

Course Highlights

The gives a very good overview of astrophysics and the various fields where research is going on. The project given us a hands on experience of data analysis in the context of physics and the professor makes sure that the course is fun.

Course Importance

The course is a must for anyone who is interested in astronomy and wants to know the various opportunities available. The research opportunities are manifold, ranging from gravitational wave detection to cosmology and deep space. People can also focus on the engineering and optics aspect including telescopes and detectors.

How strongly would I recommend this course?

The course is a must for anyone who is interested in astronomy.

When to take this course?

I took the course in my sixth semester. The course had students across several batches (second year to fifth year) so I don’t think there’s any particular ‘ideal’ time as such.

Going Forward

People can also take courses related to general relativity and can also explore the various events held by Krittika. Projects under professors (not only IIT Bombay, but also IUCAA and the like) can be taken up.

An Introduction to Modern Astrophysics - Book by Bradley W Carroll and Dale A Ostlie

PH 556 Review By: Bhavini Jeloka

We thank the Mechanical DAMP team for this review

Throw some light on the field in which your project is going on.

My BTP titled ‘Design of an in-draft supersonic wind tunnel’ involves a new method to develop supersonic flow for testing. Generally, a high pressurized chamber is used to provide the pressure factor, but it has been providing us lower test durations & is quite unsafe to work with. My project was to design the contour of the said tunnel’s nozzle, to estimate the test duration & also check the compatibility of the same with the existing department’s vacuum chamber.

Motivation to undertake this project.

I was interested in experimental fluid mechanics, particularly high speed flows. My guide presented the idea of developing such a facility in our department & I immediately signed up for it, because it involved every aspect of experimental research, starting from a conceptual design and market study, to getting the model complete.

How are you able to manage it with other courses?

Although I had 54 credits of load when I picked up the project, I was able to give an hour everyday to work on the CAD and CFD part. I used to give extra time on weekends for paper review and recalculations. Roughly 8 hrs per week is a very healthy time commitment for such a project.

How does this project help you work towards your future goals?

My upcoming DDP will be an extension of the same. Since I have the design ready, my next task will be the physical work in the lab, like setting up sensors and establishing the proof of concept of the model developed. Also I intend to test particular geometries inside the tunnel and set flow visualization methods.

Any tips regarding BTP?

Firstly decide on the stream of interest. In aerospace, we have aerodynamics, propulsion, controls and structures. Then see if you wanna work in an experimental setup based project or something computational. That should narrow down the professor list to 3 or 4 max. Then your course reviews about the prof as an instructor along with senior reviews will help you choose one suitable for you.
You can always meet any professor and ask about a potential project under them. Most of them have an ongoing project which they shall put you in. Rest is upon the mutual interest of you and your potential guide on the topic, but their research field on the whole shall give you a good learning, no matter the project be of his choice.

title: Technology, Culture and Policy
description: 1. Technology and Culture Theories: The historical-materialist view - cultural superstructure and economic base (Marx); Evolution of cultural beliefs and rationality in different societies; Separation of State and Church; The state of modern capitalist societies - Alienation, mass psychology (Freud, Frankfurt School), Technocratic elitism (Illich); Consumer societies (Baudrillard); Media ecology (Postman); The “Masculinity” of Technology; Policy as the politics of class conflict. 2a. Technology & Public Policy in Industrial Capitalism in the “Developed” World: A brief history of the industrial revolution; Wage labor, factory based production and the assembly line, economic efficiency and productivity; Growth and development, indices of growth (GDP, growth rate); Wartime developments and the rise of the military-industrial complex; The new communications and transport technologies. 2b. Technology & Public Policy in Capitalism in “Developing” Countries: Legacies of imperialism; Capitalism and socialism as rival systems, state planning vs market driven development; From self reliance (import substitution) to globalization/liberalization; Variants of capitalism in emerging economies (state, crony, unregulated, compradore …); Technology and the rise of consumerism driven “modernity”, broken/partial industrialization. 2c. Technology & Public Policy in the Globalized Era of Finance Capitalism: Decline of manufacturing and the rise of services, outsourcing of manufacturing to cheap labor zones; Emergence of industrial and consumer electronics, computers, internet, consumption of “media” and entertainment; Monopoly private corporations as service providers, regulatory conflicts (US, EU and Asia), data and social media; Casualization of labor; Artificial Intelligence, automation and unemployment; Speculative valuation of firms and shares, venture capital funded business models for start-ups. 3. The Case Studies - Each case study will be structured in 4 parts: the facilitating technology, cultural belief/ideology it engenders, policy in use/recommended/created, consequences. Only some of these may be taken up. A. The Organization of Work: i. Industrial capitalism: The assembly line, Taylorism, productivity policies, economic efficiency, working conditions, minimum wages, working hours/week. ii. Finance capitalism: The new assembly line, services outsourcing, and the cybertariat; Automation, robotics and artificial intelligence; Massive casualization of labor - the gig economy. B. Political Culture: i. Technology in Wars and Genocide - The gas chambers of the Holocaust, nuclear weapons, missiles and drones; War as entertainment and spectacle. ii. Dystopian Politics: From TV to social media, era of post-truth, “nationalist” policies, the displacement of rationality and scientific reasoning by identity based “truth”; Social media and “alternative” facts; Threats to privacy, free speech; The surveillance state. C. Everyday Life: i. Technology as Tool for Political Mobilization: Nazi belief systems, racial purity, eugenics, politics of fascism and cultural mobilization; The marriage of modern technology with reactionary ideologies, “alternative” facts, identity based “reasoning”. ii. The Mass Psychology of Dystopia: Impact of social media, gadget and the internet, “connectedness addiction”, self-obsession, self-awareness, notions of self and others, human relations in the AI universe, iii. Preserving Patriarchy: Reproductive technologies and control of women’s bodies, the “industrialization” of childbirth; Female foeticide. iv. Surrogacy in Reproduction: The breakdown of the “Family”; Child rights and maternal health; Individual rights versus the prostitution analogy.

reviews.body:
Year : 2014-15, Autumn Semester

Instructor: Prof. Ankur Kulkarni and Prof. Leena Vachhani

Motivation:

Control Systems Engineering is a multi disciplinary field which involves modelling a system (mechanical, electrical,chemical or even biological) mathematically and designing to get the desired output from it. This course mainly deals with the former part and by the end of course, you will be able to understand a system,characterise its output,comment on its stability and will get an intuitive understanding of what feedback is.

Course Content:

Feedback on Lectures

This course is taken by two Professors in two different halves and are starkly different. First half deals with Fourier,Laplace transforms and may seem like a recap of MA205 (Complex Analysis). It will require a bit of practice,the same way expected of a MA Course. No slides are provided and it is a good idea to take notes as they contain many rigorous mathematical proofs.

Second half introduces you to Control systems and their analysis. Lectures are of normal pace and the instructor focuses on mostly getting an intuitive feel of how systems behave in closed and open loop, e.g. pole location, stability etc.

Feedback on Tutorials

Since this course requires a lot of mathematics, each instructor gives 2 assignments each. Assignments are usually easy to do if you have been going to the classes regularly and contribute to the overall understanding of the course.

Exams

Exams are of normal length and if you have a good understanding of the theory and can solve the assignment questions, you are good to go.

Prerequisites: SC 201

References

Marking Scheme:

Each half of course has a weightage of 50 marks. For each half, 20 marks are for tutorials and rest 30 are for the half semester examination.

Grading Stats:

Total      49

Difficulty Level: Moderate

Advanced Courses and Applications for the future:

EE 640: Multivariable Control Systems

SC 627 – Motion Planning and Coordination of Autonomous Vehicles

Note: These courses should only be taken after completing SC 201, SC 202, SC 301 (in that order) as they help you build a foundation and understand the fundamentals of control and systems engineering.

SC 202 is most basic course in making you familiar to control theory which finds applications in automobile anti-lock braking systems, aircraft flight controllers, robotic manufacturing assembly lines, rate-adaptive pacemakers etc. Basically, you study and implement the integration of all component systems making system and control a multi-disciplinary field.

reviews.body:
Instructor: Prof. Srikant Sukumar and Prof. Arpita Sinha

Session: 2019-2020

Prerequisites: SC201 (or SC639), SC202

Course content and structure: 

The course was divided into 2 parts –
1. Linear Systems
2. Non-Linear Systems

Topics covered in Part 1 –
ODE existence and uniqueness, State transition matrix properties, Jordan decomposition, Controllability, Observability, Lyapunov functions for stability analysis, Specialization to eigenvalues for LTI case, Linear feedback – LTI pole placement via canonical forms, LQR, Output feedback.

Topics covered in Part 2 –
Local/global existence and uniqueness of solutions to nonlinear ODE’s, Lyapunov stability definitions, linearizing of non-linear systems, Phase Space Plots, equilibrium points, identifying uniform stability, uniform asymptotic stability, exponential stability and global stability, Lyapunov direct method, Lyapunov theorems for stability, Krasovskii-LaSalle theorem.

Feedback on lectures: 

Prof. Srikant taught us part 1 and Prof. Sinha taught us part 2. Their teaching styles were quite different but both of them always interacted with the students and answered doubts satisfactorily.
Srikant sir is an amazing teacher and his lectures were always fun to attend. He used to bring his handwritten notes and project them a screen. Any mistake in the notes was recognized and corrected promptly and the final version was uploaded on moodle. The only difficulty I faced in his lectures was his fast pace.
Arpita ma’am preferred using the board so we had to make our own notes. She always took some time to answer questions but made sure that all doubts were cleared. She is a decent teacher but I personally found her lectures slightly boring.

A lot of content was covered in a very short time so it was crucial to attend lectures and study the concepts regularly.

Feedback on tutorials and exams:

Grading scheme –
Part 1
Best of Quiz 1 and Quiz 2 – 25%
Midsem – 25%
Part 2
Quiz 1 – 25 %
Endsem – 25 %

All exams were open book and open notes and therefore quite difficult. Tutorials were conducted occasionally where we solved some problems and and asked our doubts.

Difficulty (on a scale of 1 being very easy to 5 being very hard): 4

Textbooks & References: 

1. Linear System Theory by Joao P. Hespanha
2. Linear Systems by Panos J. Antsaklis, Anthony N. Michel
3. Nonlinear Systems by Hassan Khalil
4. Nonlinear Systems Analysis, Volume 42 of Classics in Applied Mathematics, M. Vidyasagar

Teaching Style

Ma’am would upload lecture slides beforehand and those lectures would have the topics that were covered that week. It would also have a list of 3-5 questions that would make up the weekly assignment. The lecture hours were used for doubts (Fridays) and tutorials (Wednesdays). Ma’am followed the prescribed books in terms of the content covered so it would be easy to know what was going on in class.

Tutorials/Assignments/Projects

The tutorials were basically discussions of the assignments given. The assignments were directly taken from the questions at the back of the chapters and were generally easy to solve and would not take time. However, calculations and simplifications were often time consuming.

Feedback on Exams

Quizzes
The quiz would be on Moodle every week. The questions, while easy to solve, could go wrong due to calculation mistakes (in say, matrix multiplications). This would often get aggravated due to the tight time constraint of 10 minutes.

Midsem
The midsem only covered linear control theory and had the same difficulty level compared to the quizzes and assignments.

Endsem
The endsem focussed on nonlinear control theory and once again, had the same difficulty level compared to the quizzes and assignments.

Motivation for taking this course

The course was a part of my minor requirements and also served as a basis for advanced courses in Syscon. It is one of the few basic courses that delves deeper into control theory and the associated concepts of stability.

Course Highlights

The course covered basic linear and nonlinear control theory and will definitely help in future courses that involve controller design. The course is easy to follow and can be done well if one does the assignments properly.

Course Importance

The course is one of the primary foundation of control theory and controller design and can be applied to a wide variety of problems including robotics, spacecraft control and autonomous vehicles. Most of the courses in syscon have SC301 as their soft pre-requisites.

How strongly would I recommend this course?

I would definitely recommend anyone who wants to explore control theory to take this course as it is the foundation of systems and control.

When to take this course?

The course can be taken in the 5th semester, right after SC639 and SC202 as it will be in the recommended order and concepts will be easier to understand.

Going Forward

Taking adaptive control theory or courses based on robust or optimal control would be a good step forward. Some courses in the chemical department like process control and model predictive control would also be easier to follow after SC301. In terms of research, lots of options open in Syscon is one has a basic understanding of linear and nonlinear control.

Nonlinear Systems - Book by Hassan K Khalil

Instructor

Pre-Midsem Course Content

Since the course did not have a strict ‘mid-sem’ per say; I’ll be demarking basis the traditional timeline followed.

The first half focuses majorly on reviewing already taught concepts in MA105/MA109; i.e reviewing real-analysis. This is followed by a couple of lectures in which ‘defining’ and structuring an optimization problem is focused on. Some of the easier aspects of the course get covered such as optimization in Euclidean spaces, Linear Programming etc is covered.
For this half, revisiting some concepts from Linear Algebra and following the lectures by the professor prove more than sufficient.
Pro tip: Try to score as much as you can pre-midsem; ride gets tougher from here :p

The immediate post-midsem content was the part that I struggled the most with. These involved some concepts such as KKT conditions, optimization algorithms such as: Line search method, simplex method, Interior point method etc.

The math here does get heavy and solving assignment problems and voicing doubts out to the professor during lectures would be recommended (and something I regret not doing!)

Towards the absolute end of the course, some familiar concepts are covered such as Newtons Method, Gradient Descent etc.

SC 607- Optimization

Semester: Spring 2017

Instructor: Prof. Ankur Kulkarni

Course Credits: 6

Prerequisites: Linear algebra, real analysis and some comfort with mathematical reasoning. For UGs, consent of instructor is mandatory. The prerequisites aren’t necessarily rigid, but you should be motivated enough to take up the course.

Motivation and brief description:

The following description presented by the instructor on the course page sums up the importance of this course,

“I optimize, therefore I am

The urge to optimize is fundamental to our being. If we exist, we think. If we think, we make choices. If we make choices, we want to optimize. Indeed, optimization may also be fundamental to what makes our being. Laws governing the behavior of physical (and hence biological) systems seem to have the character of seeking to optimize some energy functional.

This course will provide an understanding of the theory of mathematical optimization and some landmark algorithms. Throughout the emphasis will be on the geometry of the problem and uncovering geometric obstructions to the success of algorithms.”

It’s needless to say that the skills learnt in this course would definitely come in handy in Electrical Engineering and allied fields.

Course Content:

Convex analysis and review of real analysis, Optimization in Euclidean space: linear programming, integer programming, bilinear programming, duality, constrained optimization, convex optimization, constraint qualification, KKT conditions, geometric theory of duality

Algorithms and convergence- Simplex method (for linear programming), Sequential convexification, lift and project method (for binary programs), Active set method (Quadratic programming), Line search method (unconstrained optimization), Constrained optimization (Barrier, Penalty, Cutting plane method, Augmented Lagrangian methods), Interior point method

Lectures:

The lectures were extremely well structured. Relevant concepts, proofs and examples were meticulously explained on the board. Lectures were very interactive and the instructor gave the students enough time to absorb the concepts. The instructor was very receptive to doubts.

Attendance:

Although attendance was not monitored, attending all lectures is highly recommended.

Evaluation:

40%   Assignments  

10%   Scribing Lecture Notes

50%   Endsem

Lecture notes:

As a part of the course, each of us was required to volunteer for taking notes of one lecture as per the prescribed format. Before endsem, all the lecture notes were made available for reference.  

Assignments:

The assignments played a very crucial part in the learning process. Each problem was an illustration of some important concept taught in the class. The difficulty was moderate and on occasions, the assignments were quite lengthy. Leaving the assignments for the last moment is discouraged.

Examination:

There was only a single end-semester exam worth 50 marks. The questions were mainly a  random selection from the homework problems and problems discussed in class with minor changes. This format was revealed beforehand. Sincerely solving assignments and going through class notes is a must for scoring well in the exam. But speed is also important as there were some questions on algorithms which demanded tedious calculations.

Grading:

Moderate grading. You can refer to Asc for more details.

For obtaining an audit grade, one was expected to submit assignments and scribe notes.

Instructor

Prof. Sukumar Srikant
